The extraordinary change since the end of last week reflects many crosscurrents. Some of the forces include foreign selling of Treasuries, with potential Chinese selling in retaliation for tariffs; investor unrest, reflected in poor Treasury auctions; and investor selling of Treasuries just to raise cash – period. And a concern that sent yields higher last year with sell-offs was related to fiscal strain in the US. On the muni side, the selling has not been related to tax concerns of munis as much as to the total volatility in the Treasury market, which has made it impossible for municipal dealers to hedge inventory. This has also been reflected in many muni deals being postponed by issuers due to market conditions. And bond fund selling has also resumed – again, most likely as a dash for cash, such as Treasuries. Longer bond yields have been beaten up much more than shorter maturities.

Clearly, some stability in the equity markets should bring some leveling from the volatility in the bond markets. Munis are certainly very cheap, in our view. The tariff talks will continue to drive bond markets over the near term. But if the economy enters a slowdown (almost certain) or a recession (getting more certain), that trajectory will have implications for municipal credit quality, as an economic slowdown will have effects on income tax levels, sales tax levels, capital gains receipts, and other revenues as well. This means staying at the highest levels of credit quality. When a recession appears, credit widening is sure to follow, and corporate spreads have already started to widen.

data.title: XDTE: Weathering The Storm With 0DTE Strategies
data.description:
Introduction

I first covered the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (XDTE) back in December last year, when I documented how well it was doing against the index itself, even outperforming it over XDTE's lifespan.

Looking as far back as we can, XDTE is still beating the index. While the market has been effectively flat since XDTE debuted, the fund has kept on top of it for the most part, and has even come out in the positive.

All the while, XDTE has served one primary function: produce weekly income.

The fund itself is a covered call fund following the S&P 500 index, which means that it is primarily long the index, and caps its upside in exchange for income. That income stream provides the majority of the gains to the fund, which we can see in the fund's divergence between price return and total return (which includes dividends paid, but not reinvested).

XDTE Strategy & Holdings

So what is going on with this fund? The strategy hasn't changed much since I last reviewed this, despite the large changes in the fund's performance, so I'm going to pull from my previous article for a quick rundown:

1) Buys long-dated ITM call options to build exposure to an underlying index, in XDTE's case, the S&P 500 through the SPX index.

2) Sells ATM call options every market open, set to expire the same day, "0DTE".

3) Closes the ATM option by market close, win or lose.

3) Aims to profit from option premium and overnight movements of the index.

4) There is some theory that says overnight market movements are more powerful than intraday movements, but past performance can only confirm so much of a strategy as unique as this.

This has led the fund to produce significant income, alongside capital gains. For the first half of its lifespan, during a bull market, the fund itself was effectively flat price-wise, while still distributing high income.

The holdings themselves are very straightforward, with the fund only having three core holdings and a cash position.

These options positions create the P&L shown in the above chart, with the goal of replicating close to 100% long exposure overnight as possible.

XDTE Dividends

Since the market has been falling, we've seen XDTE tank alongside. It still carries a yield of 24%, which is very substantial. Note that the distributions are variable, as they often coincide with how volatile the market is. More volatility means higher options premiums, which means higher income.

Volatility Brings Income

I expect our next few distributions to be elevated, as the VIX has currently run up to a new high not seen since the COVID-19 crash, and the GFC before that. Going further than that, I expect volatility to endure for some time, as trade tensions and political chaos have created a new regime.

Just note that the strategy of being long-only overnight may be in peril a bit, only because now the President is waiting until after the market-close to make announcements, like he did with his Liberation Day speech on April 2nd.

I'm mostly joking here, as the overnight strategy and 0DTE options selling is in response to overall market trends, not necessarily front-running individual events.

Suitability

One of the reasons this fund has come back up is because I have been reorienting my own holdings due to the market selloff. While I am primarily concentrating my current capital in non-equity holdings, namely managed futures, I have been reducing my equity exposure.

One way I suggest folks reduce their equity exposure without going into "sell everything" mode is to adjust their positions in covered call ETFs like XDTE. Among S&P 500 covered call ETFs, XDTE has surprised me and impressed me the most. While I still favor tax-efficient funds like SPYI, and my personal holdings are concentrated in the more value-oriented JEPI, XDTE still deserves a good consideration for income investors looking for a way to reduce some market risk, increase income, and prepare for the markets' continued volatility.

I expect volatility to continue, and so the most prudent course of action is to hold funds that benefit from that increase in volatility, like covered call funds. Those like XDTE which have outperformed the market on a total return basis, especially given its unique strategy, should be given preference over long-only indices for the time being.

That being said, XDTE is small and relatively new. I recommend aggressive investors consider no more than a 10% allocation to the fund, with moderate investors advised to consider no more than a 5% allocation. Conservative investors are better off with JEPI, and should consider that instead.

Conclusion

Ultimately, the Roundhill S&P 500 0DTE Covered Call Strategy ETF (BATS:XDTE) is positioned to take advantage of future market tensions. I don't believe there will be an easy way out of our current geopolitical drama over tariffs, which will roil markets and likely increase volatility moving forward. This leaves funds like XDTE uniquely positioned to take advantage of this, by producing income while markets swing about, and are on a flat-to-down trajectory currently.

I also believe that the Fed will not save markets, or act as a "put," meaning that I don't expect any face-melting rips from the market anytime soon, which could hamper XDTE's returns.

Investors are encouraged, however, to heed two pieces of advice that I have been giving out in many of my articles lately. They are both very prudent to consider currently, and it would behoove folks to take them seriously before they find themselves in too deep of a position in something as risky as equities.

Now seems like a great time to re-evaluate our risk tolerances.

...

Position sizing is often more important than security selection.

Thanks for reading.
